questions.question: Explain the difference between anonymous pipes and named pipes (FIFOs) in Unix.
keyPoints: Anonymous pipes are created using pipe() system call and work only between related processes (parent-child)
Named pipes (FIFOs) appear as special files in filesystem using mkfifo() and allow unrelated processes to communicate
Anonymous pipes are unidirectional; named pipes can also be accessed like regular files for bidirectional communication

questions.question: What is the purpose of the pipe() system call? Explain its parameters and return values.
keyPoints: pipe() creates an anonymous pipe and returns two file descriptors
The array parameter stores fd[0] for read end and fd[1] for write end
Returns 0 on success, -1 on failure with errno set
Used for parent-child process communication

questions.question: Describe what happens when a process writes to a pipe that has no readers. Also explain the scenario when reading from an empty pipe.
keyPoints: Writing to pipe with no readers causes SIGPIPE signal or EPIPE error from write()
This signals the writer that the pipe has no consumers
Reading from empty pipe blocks until data is available (unless O_NONBLOCK)
When all writers close, read() returns 0 (EOF)

questions.question: Explain the use of dup() and dup2() system calls in the context of pipes.
keyPoints: dup() duplicates a file descriptor to the lowest available number
dup2() duplicates to a specific file descriptor number
Used to redirect stdin/stdout to pipe ends
Common pattern: dup2(pipefd[1], STDOUT_FILENO) to redirect output to pipe

questions.question: Write the steps involved in creating a pipe between a parent and child process for inter-process communication.
keyPoints: Parent calls pipe() to create pipe before fork()
Parent forks to create child process
Child closes unused end (e.g., read end if sending)
Parent closes unused end (e.g., write end if receiving)
Communication proceeds using read/write on respective ends
Close ends when done to signal EOF

questions.question: What is PIPE_BUF? Why is it important in pipe operations?
keyPoints: PIPE_BUF is the maximum number of bytes guaranteed to be written atomically
Typically 4096 bytes on most Unix systems
Writes smaller than PIPE_BUF are guaranteed to be atomic
Larger writes may be interleaved with other process writes
Ensures data integrity in concurrent pipe operations

questions.question: Explain how shell pipelines (e.g., cmd1 | cmd2) work internally.
keyPoints: Shell creates a pipe using pipe() system call
Shell forks two child processes
In first child: redirects stdout to pipe write end, closes unnecessary ends
In second child: redirects stdin to pipe read end, closes unnecessary ends
Both children exec the respective commands
Parent closes both pipe ends and waits for children

questions.question: Write a C program that demonstrates communication between parent and child processes using pipes. The child should send its process ID to the parent.
keyPoints: Create pipe using pipe() before fork()
Fork to create child process
Child: close read end, convert PID to string, write to pipe, close write end
Parent: close write end, read from pipe, display received PID, close read end
Proper error handling for fork(), pipe(), read(), write()
Wait for child process using wait()
Correct use of file descriptor management

questions.question: Explain named pipes (FIFOs) in detail. How do they differ from anonymous pipes? Write a program demonstrating communication between two unrelated processes using FIFO.
keyPoints: FIFO created using mkfifo() or mknod()
Appears as special file in filesystem (ls -l shows 'p' type)
Unrelated processes can open and use it like regular file
Named pipe remains in filesystem until unlinked
Need to open FIFO at both ends for communication
Demonstrate with two separate programs: writer and reader
Both processes open FIFO, one writes, one reads
Handle blocking open() appropriately

questions.question: Write a complete C program that implements a simple shell pipeline: it should execute 'ls | wc -l' functionality using pipe() system calls.
keyPoints: Create pipe using pipe()
Fork first child for 'ls' command
First child: redirect stdout to pipe write end, close unnecessary ends, exec 'ls'
Fork second child for 'wc' command
Second child: redirect stdin to pipe read end, close unnecessary ends, exec 'wc -l'
Parent closes both pipe ends, waits for both children
Proper error handling throughout
Use dup2() for redirection
Use waitpid() or wait() for child processes

questions.question: Discuss the various pipe attributes and limitations in Unix/Linux including buffering, atomicity, and process relationships.
keyPoints: Default buffer size is 65536 bytes in modern Linux
PIPE_BUF (4096 bytes) guarantees atomic writes
Pipes are unidirectional - need two for bidirectional
Only work between related processes (anonymous) or any processes (FIFO)
Data flow is byte stream, not message boundaries
Reading is FIFO ordered
Kernel maintains pipe buffer; read removes data from buffer
Closing write end sends EOF to reader
Pipe ends are inherited across fork()
Maximum number of pipes per process limited by RLIMIT_NOFILE
